text: Canine atopic dermatitis (CAD) is a genetically predisposed, inflammatory and pruritic allergic skin disease with characteristic clinical features most commonly associated with IgE antibodies to environmental allergens. It affects approximately 10–15% of the dog population and is a leading cause of veterinary dermatology consultations. Predisposed breeds include West Highland White Terriers, Golden Retrievers, Labrador Retrievers, Bulldogs, and German Shepherd Dogs. Clinical signs include pruritus affecting the face, paws, ventral abdomen, axillae, and ears. Lesions include erythema, lichenification, hyperpigmentation, and self-induced alopecia from chronic scratching. Diagnosis is clinical, based on the Favrot criteria.

metadata.title: Canine atopic dermatitis: pathogenesis, diagnosis, and therapeutic options
metadata.authors: Olivry T, DeBoer DJ, Favrot C

text: Management of canine atopic dermatitis involves a multimodal approach targeting allergen avoidance, skin barrier restoration, and immunomodulation. Oclacitinib (Apoquel), a JAK1 inhibitor, provides rapid itch relief within 4 hours and is approved for long-term use. Lokivetmab (Cytopoint), a monoclonal antibody targeting canine IL-31, provides itch control for 4–8 weeks per injection. Cyclosporine (Atopica) is effective for long-term management. Allergen-specific immunotherapy (ASIT), administered subcutaneously or sublingually, is the only therapy that modifies the underlying immune response and is recommended as adjunctive treatment when allergens are identified. Topical therapies including ceramide-containing shampoos and sprays improve skin barrier function.

metadata.title: Canine atopic dermatitis: pathogenesis, diagnosis, and therapeutic options
metadata.authors: Olivry T, DeBoer DJ, Favrot C

text: Malassezia dermatitis, caused by the commensal yeast Malassezia pachydermatis, is a common secondary skin disease in dogs that arises when the host's cutaneous immune response is altered by predisposing conditions such as allergic skin disease, hypothyroidism, or sebaceous adenitis. Predisposed breeds include Basset Hounds, West Highland White Terriers, and Cocker Spaniels. Clinical signs include intense pruritus, malodour, greasy seborrhoea, erythema, and hyperpigmentation with a predilection for skin folds, ventral neck, axillae, and interdigital spaces. Cytology (tape strip, cotton swab, or impression smear) revealing >2 organisms per high-power field supports the diagnosis. Treatment includes antifungal shampoos (miconazole/chlorhexidine) and systemic azoles (ketoconazole, fluconazole) for 3–4 weeks.

metadata.title: Malassezia dermatitis in dogs: epidemiology, diagnosis, and antifungal management
metadata.authors: Bond R, Morris DO, Guillot J

text: Canine superficial pyoderma is a bacterial skin infection involving the superficial layers of the skin and hair follicles, most commonly caused by Staphylococcus pseudintermedius. It is almost invariably secondary to an underlying cause such as atopic dermatitis, demodicosis, hypothyroidism, or iatrogenic immunosuppression. Clinical lesions include papules, pustules, epidermal collarettes, and focal alopecia. Pruritus is variable. Cytology of intact pustules reveals neutrophils and intracellular cocci. Treatment involves topical antiseptic therapy (chlorhexidine 2–4% shampoos or sprays) as a first-line approach. Systemic antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate, cephalexin, or trimethoprim-sulphonamide) are reserved for extensive or recurrent cases. Culture and sensitivity testing is recommended for recurrent pyoderma to identify methicillin-resistant S. pseudintermedius (MRSP).

metadata.title: Canine superficial pyoderma: bacteriology, treatment strategies, and antimicrobial stewardship

text: Feline miliary dermatitis is a clinical reaction pattern characterized by small, crusted papules distributed over the dorsal trunk, neck, and head. It is not a specific diagnosis but a clinical presentation associated with multiple underlying causes, most commonly hypersensitivity reactions (flea allergy dermatitis, food allergy, atopic dermatitis) and ectoparasite infestations. Flea allergy dermatitis is the most frequent cause globally; even cats with no visible fleas may react to infrequent exposures. Pruritus is often intense. Diagnosis requires systematic elimination of underlying causes. Rigorous flea control (all in-contact animals treated, environmental treatment) is the first step. Food elimination trials with a novel protein or hydrolyzed diet for 8–12 weeks rule out food hypersensitivity. Glucocorticoids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day, tapered) or oclacitinib provide symptomatic relief during investigation.

metadata.title: Feline miliary dermatitis: a systematic approach to diagnosis and management
metadata.journal: Journal of Feline Medicine and Surgery
metadata.authors: Hobi S, Linek M, Marignac G
